romania.title: Romgaz anunță că a ajuns la un acord de principiu pentru preluarea Azomureș. Când se vor finaliza negocierile
romania.summary: Producătorul de gaze Romgaz a anunțat miercuri că a ajuns la un acord de principiu cu Azomureș privind principalii termeni comerciali ai tranzacției de preluare a activității operaționale a combinatului chimic, prin transfer de afacere. Finalizarea negocierilor este estimată până la sfârșitul lunii. „SNGN Romgaz SA informează acționarii și investitorii că, în urma negocierilor purtate …

romania.title: Svaneborg Kardyb aduc jazzul electronic nordic la București
romania.summary: Duo-ul danez Svaneborg Kardyb, unul dintre cele mai apreciate nume ale noului val de jazz nordic contemporan, revine la București într-un concert de neratat – joia aceasta, 7 mai, după… Articolul Svaneborg Kardyb aduc jazzul electronic nordic la București apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: Shakespeare School lansează „Zilele Examenelor Cambridge” – workshopuri gratuite pentru părinți și elevi (8–19 ani)
romania.summary: Shakespeare School, desemnat „Cel mai bun centru de pregătire a examenelor Cambridge din România” de către Cambridge University Press & Assessment, organizează în perioada 12-14 mai 2026 Zilele Examenelor Cambridge… Articolul Shakespeare School lansează „Zilele Examenelor Cambridge” – workshopuri gratuite pentru părinți și elevi (8–19 ani) apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.summary: Unică din punct de vedere al demersului și metodologiei, în cultura și psihanaliza din România, cartea A fost odată ca niciodată… Eseuri de psihanaliză a basmului românesc își introduce cititorii… Articolul Petre Ispirescu, legătura dintre viața și psihologia basmelor sale, în cartea „A fost odată ca niciodată… Eseuri de psihanaliză a basmului românesc” apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: Andreia Bodea, directoarea Colegiului I.L.Caragiale: „Văd scene în birou în care elevul îi spune mamei: <<Hai, mă lași cu astea?>>. Acum 10 ani, așa ceva nu se întâmpla.”
romania.summary: Relația dintre (pre)adolescenți, părinți și școală pare, astăzi, mai fragilă ca oricând. Între dorința copiilor de autonomie, nevoia părinților de control și presiunea unui sistem educațional care încearcă să țină pasul cu schimbările sociale, apar tensiuni, neînțelegeri și, uneori, rupturi greu de reparat. Andreia Bodea, directoarea Colegiului Național „Ion Luca Caragiale” din București, vorbește într-un …

Arătând că e un adevărat om de acţiune, acesta a anunţat că îşi întrerupe urgent vizita în Armenia pentru a pleca undeva şi mai departe. „Având în vedere ultimele evoluţii din politica de […] The post Breaking: Nicuşor Dan şi-a scurtat vizita în Armenia ca să plece undeva şi mai departe appeared first on Times New Roman .

În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

romania.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
romania.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

medical.title: Can AI forecast immune responses? New benchmark shows where predictions hold up and fail
medical.summary: Artificial intelligence is increasingly being used to help scientists accelerate drug discovery and search for new treatments. But for AI tools to work effectively, researchers need to know whether they can be validated and applied in real-world situations.

medical.title: Hidden reservoir may drive brain cancer relapse in 65% of patients
medical.summary: All types of brain cancers collectively have a five-year survival rate of just 33%, according to the National Cancer Institute's Surveillance, Epidemiology, and End Results Program. For a type of brain cancer called glioblastoma, however, the five-year survival rate is 7%, according to Sara G. M. Piccirillo, Ph.D., a tenure-track assistant professor in the Department of Cell Biology and Physiology at The University of New Mexico School of Medicine and a full member of the UNM Comprehensive Cancer Center.

medical.title: 'I'm mad at the people who could have solved the problem': What kids say about eco‑anxiety
medical.summary: "This is our home. If we destroy it, and we can't build it up, then that's a part of Earth that's destroyed, and we won't be able to get it back." Matthew, aged 10, isn't alone in feeling this way.

medical.title: Even in Japan, robots are a long way from being fully fledged caregivers—here's why
medical.summary: The robot pauses at the edge of the room as an engineer checks its sensors. Then, with a soft mechanical hum, this humanoid machine begins to move. It lifts a mannequin from a bed, slowly and carefully. The engineers hold their breath.

medical.title: Large study finds a strong link between depression and cannabis use
medical.summary: A new meta-analysis of 55 studies involving more than 3 million people has found that 31% of individuals with cannabis use disorder (CUD) also struggle with major depressive disorder (MDD). While a link between these two conditions has been known for some time, this study provides the clearest evidence to date that the relationship goes both ways. CUD was also found to be present in 10% of those with MDD.

medical.title: While patients lay unconscious under anesthesia, their brains kept decoding stories and preparing for what came next
medical.summary: Baylor College of Medicine researchers have found that the human brain is capable of sophisticated language processing while in an unconscious state from general anesthesia. The findings, published in Nature, challenge what we know about the role of consciousness and cognition, and could open new ways of understanding memory, language and brain-computer interfaces.

medical.title: Vitamin C and cancer: Was Nobel laureate Linus Pauling on to something?
medical.summary: Linus Pauling was one of the most brilliant scientists of the 20th century. He won two Nobel prizes and transformed our understanding of chemical bonds and the structure of proteins. Late in his career, though, he became famous for something very different: a passionate belief that very high doses of vitamin C could help people with cancer. Many doctors scoffed. When Pauling himself later died of cancer aged 93, he was held up as a classic case of the "halo effect": being a genius in one field doesn't guarantee wisdom in another.

medical.title: Artery widening, not blockages, linked to common stroke
medical.summary: Scientists have uncovered new evidence that challenges long-held assumptions about the causes of a common type of stroke, offering clues as to why widely used treatments may not work. The study found that the buildup of fatty deposits in arteries does not appear to cause lacunar ischemic stroke, which accounts for around a quarter of all ischemic strokes—strokes caused by a blocked blood vessel—in the UK each year.

medical.title: You don’t need intense workouts to build muscle, new study reveals
medical.summary: Building muscle doesn’t have to mean exhausting workouts or soreness. Researchers found that slow, controlled “lowering” movements can boost strength more efficiently while requiring less effort. Even five minutes a day of simple exercises like chair squats or wall push-ups can make a real difference. It’s a smarter, easier way to get stronger—no gym required.

medical.title: Scientists discover hidden “master switch” driving skin cancer growth and immune escape
medical.summary: A key protein, HOXD13, helps melanoma tumors grow and evade the immune system by boosting blood supply and blocking cancer-fighting T cells. Disabling it shrinks tumors and reopens the door for the immune system—offering a new path for treatment.

medical.title: Scientists discover skincare compound that kills drug-resistant bacteria
medical.summary: A popular Korean skincare ingredient may be far more powerful than anyone realized. Scientists have discovered that madecassic acid—derived from the herb Centella asiatica—can stop antibiotic-resistant bacteria in their tracks, including dangerous strains of E. coli. By targeting a bacterial protein that humans don’t have, the compound disrupts the microbes’ ability to survive, making it a promising new type of antibiotic.

science.title: Portable sensor detects PFAS in water on-site, cutting need for costly lab tests
science.summary: A new study has unveiled a new method to cost-effectively and practically test for "forever chemicals" in water, potentially revolutionizing environmental PFAS monitoring. Led by Griffith University, the novel PFAS detection technique is a portable sensor designed to provide rapid, highly sensitive, and selective onsite testing, offering a practical alternative to laboratory-only analysis. The study, "Molecularly imprinted polyaniline-functionalized lateral-flow membrane for highly sensitive and selective per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances detection in water," has been published in Environmental Science & Technology.

science.title: Why we need to treat Earth like a spaceship
science.summary: Four humans recently looped around the moon. Their vessel, an Artemis capsule, was a thin metal shell whose life-support system kept them alive: it provided a carefully balanced atmosphere, a closed water loop, a finite supply of food, and a means for disposing of human waste. The life support was not optional. It was a necessity.

science.title: Buried in Arnhem Land, an ancient fire trick may rewrite early stone technology's timeline
science.summary: A recent archaeological study has identified the earliest lithic heat treatment of chert in the world. Discovered in Australia, this discovery is nearly twice as old as any previously identified chert heat treatment in Eurasia. The study is published in the Journal of Paleolithic Archaeology.

science.title: 'What do you want to be?' The spark that helps Indigenous people go to university
science.summary: Across Australia, universities and governments say increasing the numbers of Indigenous graduates is one of the main priorities in tertiary education.

science.title: Heat‑resistant corals could help reefs adapt to climate change
science.summary: Austin Bowden-Kerby, a pioneer in coral reef conservation, spends many of his days gardening corals for reefs around Fiji and the Pacific. He grows corals in ocean nurseries. Once they're healthy enough, he moves them to outer ocean areas with the hope they will replicate and grow.

science.title: Federal grant terminations disproportionately impact minority scientists, study finds
science.summary: Researchers from University of California San Diego Herbert Wertheim School of Public Health and Human Longevity Science have found that recent federal grant terminations targeting research on health equity and gender identity have disproportionately affected scientists from the very communities those studies aim to support. The findings were published May 5, 2026 in The Lancet Regional Health—Americas.

science.title: 'Not just hot water': Marine heat waves can create toxic relationship between seagrasses and microbes
science.summary: Heat stress from marine heat waves can create a toxic relationship between seagrasses and a hidden ecosystem of bacteria, transforming a previously beneficial co-existence between marine plants and microbes into a harmful one, a University of Sydney and UNSW study has found. The research is published in New Phytologist.

science.title: 'Much‑needed fresh air': 5 outcomes from the world's first summit on ending fossil fuels
science.summary: Almost 60 countries, representing about a third of the global economy, met in the Colombian port city of Santa Marta for the first international summit on the transition away from fossil fuels.

science.title: School cell phone bans deliver benefits—but not right away
science.summary: New research reveals that while bans aren't an instant panacea for problems in U.S. classrooms, schools can achieve positive outcomes with persistence, according to a report published by the National Bureau of Economic Research.

science.title: This 275-million-year-old animal had a twisted jaw like nothing alive today
science.summary: Deep in a dried-up riverbed in Brazil, scientists uncovered a bizarre prehistoric mystery—twisted jawbones from a strange, long-lost animal unlike anything seen before. Dating back 275 million years, this creature, named Tanyka amnicola, belonged to an ancient lineage that should have already faded away, making it a kind of “living fossil” of its time.

science.title: This “Pink Floyd” spider hunts prey 6x its size and lives in walls
science.summary: Scientists have uncovered a tiny wall-dwelling spider named Pikelinia floydmuraria, inspired by Pink Floyd. Despite its size, it’s a fierce predator that hunts ants much larger than itself and helps reduce common urban pests like mosquitoes and flies. Its clever strategy of building webs near lights makes it especially effective. The discovery also raises new questions about its mysterious link to similar spiders in the Galápagos.

science.title: Oxford physicists achieve first-ever “quadsqueezing” breakthrough in quantum physics
science.summary: Scientists have created a powerful new way to control quantum systems, achieving the first-ever demonstration of quadsqueezing—an elusive fourth-order quantum effect. By combining simple forces in a clever way, they made previously hidden quantum behaviors visible and usable, opening new frontiers for quantum technology.

science.title: This new aluminum could replace rare metals and cut costs dramatically
science.summary: A team at King’s College London has created a powerful new aluminum compound capable of doing the work of expensive rare metals. Its unique triangular structure gives it remarkable stability and reactivity, allowing it to drive chemical reactions in ways never seen before. The discovery could lead to greener and far more affordable industrial processes. It may even enable the creation of entirely new materials.

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
mix_items.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Can AI forecast immune responses? New benchmark shows where predictions hold up and fail
mix_items.summary: Artificial intelligence is increasingly being used to help scientists accelerate drug discovery and search for new treatments. But for AI tools to work effectively, researchers need to know whether they can be validated and applied in real-world situations.

mix_items.title: Portable sensor detects PFAS in water on-site, cutting need for costly lab tests
mix_items.summary: A new study has unveiled a new method to cost-effectively and practically test for "forever chemicals" in water, potentially revolutionizing environmental PFAS monitoring. Led by Griffith University, the novel PFAS detection technique is a portable sensor designed to provide rapid, highly sensitive, and selective onsite testing, offering a practical alternative to laboratory-only analysis. The study, "Molecularly imprinted polyaniline-functionalized lateral-flow membrane for highly sensitive and selective per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances detection in water," has been published in Environmental Science & Technology.

mix_items.title: Hidden reservoir may drive brain cancer relapse in 65% of patients
mix_items.summary: All types of brain cancers collectively have a five-year survival rate of just 33%, according to the National Cancer Institute's Surveillance, Epidemiology, and End Results Program. For a type of brain cancer called glioblastoma, however, the five-year survival rate is 7%, according to Sara G. M. Piccirillo, Ph.D., a tenure-track assistant professor in the Department of Cell Biology and Physiology at The University of New Mexico School of Medicine and a full member of the UNM Comprehensive Cancer Center.

mix_items.title: Why we need to treat Earth like a spaceship
mix_items.summary: Four humans recently looped around the moon. Their vessel, an Artemis capsule, was a thin metal shell whose life-support system kept them alive: it provided a carefully balanced atmosphere, a closed water loop, a finite supply of food, and a means for disposing of human waste. The life support was not optional. It was a necessity.

mix_items.title: Buried in Arnhem Land, an ancient fire trick may rewrite early stone technology's timeline
mix_items.summary: A recent archaeological study has identified the earliest lithic heat treatment of chert in the world. Discovered in Australia, this discovery is nearly twice as old as any previously identified chert heat treatment in Eurasia. The study is published in the Journal of Paleolithic Archaeology.

mix_items.title: 'I'm mad at the people who could have solved the problem': What kids say about eco‑anxiety
mix_items.summary: "This is our home. If we destroy it, and we can't build it up, then that's a part of Earth that's destroyed, and we won't be able to get it back." Matthew, aged 10, isn't alone in feeling this way.

mix_items.title: 'What do you want to be?' The spark that helps Indigenous people go to university
mix_items.summary: Across Australia, universities and governments say increasing the numbers of Indigenous graduates is one of the main priorities in tertiary education.

mix_items.title: Heat‑resistant corals could help reefs adapt to climate change
mix_items.summary: Austin Bowden-Kerby, a pioneer in coral reef conservation, spends many of his days gardening corals for reefs around Fiji and the Pacific. He grows corals in ocean nurseries. Once they're healthy enough, he moves them to outer ocean areas with the hope they will replicate and grow.

mix_items.title: Federal grant terminations disproportionately impact minority scientists, study finds
mix_items.summary: Researchers from University of California San Diego Herbert Wertheim School of Public Health and Human Longevity Science have found that recent federal grant terminations targeting research on health equity and gender identity have disproportionately affected scientists from the very communities those studies aim to support. The findings were published May 5, 2026 in The Lancet Regional Health—Americas.

mix_items.title: Even in Japan, robots are a long way from being fully fledged caregivers—here's why
mix_items.summary: The robot pauses at the edge of the room as an engineer checks its sensors. Then, with a soft mechanical hum, this humanoid machine begins to move. It lifts a mannequin from a bed, slowly and carefully. The engineers hold their breath.

mix_items.title: 'Not just hot water': Marine heat waves can create toxic relationship between seagrasses and microbes
mix_items.summary: Heat stress from marine heat waves can create a toxic relationship between seagrasses and a hidden ecosystem of bacteria, transforming a previously beneficial co-existence between marine plants and microbes into a harmful one, a University of Sydney and UNSW study has found. The research is published in New Phytologist.

mix_items.title: 'Much‑needed fresh air': 5 outcomes from the world's first summit on ending fossil fuels
mix_items.summary: Almost 60 countries, representing about a third of the global economy, met in the Colombian port city of Santa Marta for the first international summit on the transition away from fossil fuels.

mix_items.title: Large study finds a strong link between depression and cannabis use
mix_items.summary: A new meta-analysis of 55 studies involving more than 3 million people has found that 31% of individuals with cannabis use disorder (CUD) also struggle with major depressive disorder (MDD). While a link between these two conditions has been known for some time, this study provides the clearest evidence to date that the relationship goes both ways. CUD was also found to be present in 10% of those with MDD.

mix_items.title: While patients lay unconscious under anesthesia, their brains kept decoding stories and preparing for what came next
mix_items.summary: Baylor College of Medicine researchers have found that the human brain is capable of sophisticated language processing while in an unconscious state from general anesthesia. The findings, published in Nature, challenge what we know about the role of consciousness and cognition, and could open new ways of understanding memory, language and brain-computer interfaces.

mix_items.title: School cell phone bans deliver benefits—but not right away
mix_items.summary: New research reveals that while bans aren't an instant panacea for problems in U.S. classrooms, schools can achieve positive outcomes with persistence, according to a report published by the National Bureau of Economic Research.

mix_items.title: Vitamin C and cancer: Was Nobel laureate Linus Pauling on to something?
mix_items.summary: Linus Pauling was one of the most brilliant scientists of the 20th century. He won two Nobel prizes and transformed our understanding of chemical bonds and the structure of proteins. Late in his career, though, he became famous for something very different: a passionate belief that very high doses of vitamin C could help people with cancer. Many doctors scoffed. When Pauling himself later died of cancer aged 93, he was held up as a classic case of the "halo effect": being a genius in one field doesn't guarantee wisdom in another.

mix_items.title: Artery widening, not blockages, linked to common stroke
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered new evidence that challenges long-held assumptions about the causes of a common type of stroke, offering clues as to why widely used treatments may not work. The study found that the buildup of fatty deposits in arteries does not appear to cause lacunar ischemic stroke, which accounts for around a quarter of all ischemic strokes—strokes caused by a blocked blood vessel—in the UK each year.

mix_items.title: You don’t need intense workouts to build muscle, new study reveals
mix_items.summary: Building muscle doesn’t have to mean exhausting workouts or soreness. Researchers found that slow, controlled “lowering” movements can boost strength more efficiently while requiring less effort. Even five minutes a day of simple exercises like chair squats or wall push-ups can make a real difference. It’s a smarter, easier way to get stronger—no gym required.

mix_items.title: This 275-million-year-old animal had a twisted jaw like nothing alive today
mix_items.summary: Deep in a dried-up riverbed in Brazil, scientists uncovered a bizarre prehistoric mystery—twisted jawbones from a strange, long-lost animal unlike anything seen before. Dating back 275 million years, this creature, named Tanyka amnicola, belonged to an ancient lineage that should have already faded away, making it a kind of “living fossil” of its time.

mix_items.title: This “Pink Floyd” spider hunts prey 6x its size and lives in walls
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered a tiny wall-dwelling spider named Pikelinia floydmuraria, inspired by Pink Floyd. Despite its size, it’s a fierce predator that hunts ants much larger than itself and helps reduce common urban pests like mosquitoes and flies. Its clever strategy of building webs near lights makes it especially effective. The discovery also raises new questions about its mysterious link to similar spiders in the Galápagos.

mix_items.title: Oxford physicists achieve first-ever “quadsqueezing” breakthrough in quantum physics
mix_items.summary: Scientists have created a powerful new way to control quantum systems, achieving the first-ever demonstration of quadsqueezing—an elusive fourth-order quantum effect. By combining simple forces in a clever way, they made previously hidden quantum behaviors visible and usable, opening new frontiers for quantum technology.

mix_items.title: This new aluminum could replace rare metals and cut costs dramatically
mix_items.summary: A team at King’s College London has created a powerful new aluminum compound capable of doing the work of expensive rare metals. Its unique triangular structure gives it remarkable stability and reactivity, allowing it to drive chemical reactions in ways never seen before. The discovery could lead to greener and far more affordable industrial processes. It may even enable the creation of entirely new materials.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden “master switch” driving skin cancer growth and immune escape
mix_items.summary: A key protein, HOXD13, helps melanoma tumors grow and evade the immune system by boosting blood supply and blocking cancer-fighting T cells. Disabling it shrinks tumors and reopens the door for the immune system—offering a new path for treatment.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover skincare compound that kills drug-resistant bacteria
mix_items.summary: A popular Korean skincare ingredient may be far more powerful than anyone realized. Scientists have discovered that madecassic acid—derived from the herb Centella asiatica—can stop antibiotic-resistant bacteria in their tracks, including dangerous strains of E. coli. By targeting a bacterial protein that humans don’t have, the compound disrupts the microbes’ ability to survive, making it a promising new type of antibiotic.

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: Ce nivel de motivatie ai azi? - Decorativ.

satire_ro.title: Directorul Poştei Române, despre noua siglă a lui Dinamo: E bestială!
